1:
"And when Balaam saw that it pleased the LORD to bless Israel, he went not, as at other times, to seek for enchantments, but he set his face toward the wilderness." The very brief verse notes Balaam ceasing from enchantments and turning to the wilderness. The grammatical voices note the change. Henry and Gill note that "when Balaam saw that it pleased the LORD to bless Israel, he went not, as at other times, to seek for enchantments, but he set his face toward the wilderness" — seeing that God would bless Israel, Balaam ceased seeking omens and looked toward the camp; the it pleased the LORD to bless Israel (God's purpose to bless, twice declared); the he went not, as at other times, to seek for enchantments (Balaam ceasing from divination); the he set his face toward the wilderness (turning to the camp of Israel); the figure (the diviner forced to own God's purpose, the enchantments useless against the blessing; the truth that no divination prevails against God's people, "there is no enchantment against Jacob," Numbers 23:23; the figure of the powerlessness of all sorcery before God's blessing; Balaam now waiting for the Spirit rather than seeking omens, vv.2).

On "Balaam saw that it pleased the LORD to bless Israel," the witnesses note God's purpose. Gill notes Balaam seeing God's will to bless (the purpose owned, Numbers 23:23) — the blessing (God's purpose).

On "he went not, as at other times, to seek for enchantments, but he set his face toward the wilderness," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the change: "he went not... to seek for enchantments, but he set his face toward the wilderness" — Balaam ceasing from divination, turning to the camp (the enchantments useless); the figure (no sorcery against the blessing); the providence (the change, the Spirit coming upon him, vv.2). The verse displays Balaam ceasing from enchantments.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the change — "he went not, as at other times, to seek for enchantments, but he set his face toward the wilderness," the change (Henry, Gill); "Balaam saw that it pleased the LORD to bless Israel," God's purpose (Numbers 23:23, Gill); "he set his face toward the wilderness," the change (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the change. The verse marks Balaam: ceasing. The verse displays the enchantments: abandoned. The verse displays Israel: to be blessed. The verse displays the purpose: of God. The change displays the powerlessness of sorcery before the blessing. The verse displays the diviner: yielding. The verse displays the blessing: sure. The verse displays the camp: beheld. Balaam ceasing from enchantments displays the powerlessness of sorcery: he went not, as at other times, to seek for enchantments (Numbers 23:23) — the diviner forced to own God's purpose to bless, the enchantments useless against the blessing, no sorcery prevailing against God's people. The verse displays the Spirit of God coming upon Balaam (v.2).
2:
"And Balaam lifted up his eyes, and he saw Israel abiding in his tents according to their tribes; and the spirit of God came upon him." The very brief verse notes the Spirit of God coming upon Balaam as he saw Israel encamped. The grammatical voices note the Spirit. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Balaam lifted up his eyes, and he saw Israel abiding in his tents according to their tribes; and the spirit of God came upon him" — beholding Israel orderly encamped by tribes, the Spirit of God came upon Balaam to prophesy; the he saw Israel abiding in his tents according to their tribes (the orderly camp, the tribes in array); the the spirit of God came upon him (the Spirit moving the prophet); the figure (the Spirit of God using even Balaam to bless Israel, the unwilling prophet moved to true prophecy; the orderly camp a goodly sight, the tribes in their God-appointed order; the truth that God put his word even in the mouth of the hireling diviner, the Spirit overruling; the figure of the sovereign Spirit who speaks through whom he will, the blessing coming by the Spirit, not by enchantment).

On "Balaam lifted up his eyes, and he saw Israel abiding in his tents according to their tribes," the witnesses note the camp. Gill notes Israel orderly encamped by tribes (the goodly sight) — the camp (the tribes in order).

On "and the spirit of God came upon him," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the Spirit: "the spirit of God came upon him" — the Spirit moving Balaam to prophesy (the prophet overruled); the figure (the sovereign Spirit speaking through whom he will); the providence (the Spirit, the third oracle to follow, vv.3-9). The verse displays the Spirit of God coming upon Balaam.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Spirit — "he saw Israel abiding in his tents according to their tribes; and the spirit of God came upon him," the Spirit (Keil, Gill); "Balaam lifted up his eyes, and he saw Israel abiding in his tents according to their tribes," the camp (Gill); "and the spirit of God came upon him," the Spirit (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Spirit. The verse marks Israel: encamped. The verse displays the tribes: in order. The verse displays the Spirit: came upon him. The verse displays Balaam: moved. The Spirit displays God speaking through the unwilling prophet. The verse displays the camp: orderly. The verse displays the Spirit: moving. The verse displays the blessing: by the Spirit. The Spirit of God coming upon Balaam displays God's sovereign use of the prophet: the spirit of God came upon him — the Spirit moving even the hireling diviner to true prophecy, the blessing coming by the Spirit and not by enchantment, God speaking through whom he will. The verse displays the third oracle, Balaam's words (vv.3-4).
3:
"And he took up his parable, and said, Balaam the son of Beor hath said, and the man whose eyes are open hath said:" The very brief verse notes the opening of Balaam's third oracle. The grammatical voices note the parable. Clarke and Gill note that "he took up his parable, and said, Balaam the son of Beor hath said, and the man whose eyes are open hath said" — Balaam began his oracle, declaring himself the man whose eyes were opened to see; the he took up his parable (the prophetic oracle begun); the Balaam the son of Beor hath said (the speaker named); the the man whose eyes are open hath said (the opened eyes, the prophetic vision); the figure (the prophet whose eyes were opened by God to see the truth, though against his will; the irony that Balaam, who would not see the angel (Numbers 22:31), now has his eyes opened to behold Israel's blessing; the truth that God opens the eyes of whom he will to see his purpose; the figure of the seer, the opened eyes that behold the vision of the Almighty, vv.4).

On "he took up his parable, and said, Balaam the son of Beor hath said," the witnesses note the oracle. Gill notes Balaam taking up his parable (the oracle begun) — the parable (the oracle).

On "and the man whose eyes are open hath said," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the opened eyes: "the man whose eyes are open hath said" — the prophetic vision, the eyes opened (Numbers 22:31); the figure (God opening the eyes to see his purpose); the providence (the opened eyes, the vision of the Almighty to follow, vv.4). The verse displays the opening of Balaam's third oracle.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the parable — "he took up his parable, and said... the man whose eyes are open hath said," the parable (Clarke, Gill); "Balaam the son of Beor hath said," the speaker (Gill); "the man whose eyes are open hath said," the opened eyes (Numbers 22:31, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the parable. The verse marks the oracle: begun. The verse displays Balaam: the seer. The verse displays the eyes: opened. The verse displays the vision: given. The parable displays the prophet's opened eyes. The verse displays the oracle: taken up. The verse displays the seer: speaking. The verse displays the eyes: open. The opening of Balaam's third oracle displays the opened eyes of the seer: the man whose eyes are open hath said (Numbers 22:31) — Balaam declaring himself the seer whose eyes God had opened to behold the truth, though against his will, God opening the eyes of whom he will. The verse displays the vision of the Almighty (v.4).
4:
"He hath said, which heard the words of God, which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open:" The very brief verse notes Balaam hearing God's words and seeing the vision of the Almighty. The grammatical voices note the vision. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he hath said, which heard the words of God, which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open" — Balaam claimed to hear God's words and see the vision of the Almighty, in a trance with eyes opened; the which heard the words of God (the words received); the which saw the vision of the Almighty (the vision); the falling into a trance, but having his eyes open (the prophetic ecstasy, the opened eyes); the figure (the prophet hearing and seeing by God's gift, the vision of the Almighty given even to Balaam; the truth that the words and the vision were God's, not the diviner's own; the figure of the revelation that comes from God, the prophet but the mouth; the irony of the opened eyes given to the covetous seer, that he might bless whom he would have cursed).

On "he hath said, which heard the words of God," the witnesses note the words. Gill notes Balaam hearing God's words (the revelation received) — the words (heard of God).

On "which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the vision: "which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open" — the vision and the trance (the prophetic ecstasy); the figure (the revelation from God, the prophet the mouth); the providence (the vision, the blessing of the tents to follow, vv.5). The verse displays Balaam hearing God's words and seeing the vision.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the vision — "which heard the words of God, which saw the vision of the Almighty," the vision (Keil, Gill); "he hath said, which heard the words of God," the words (Gill); "which saw the vision of the Almighty, falling into a trance, but having his eyes open," the vision (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vision. The verse marks the words: of God. The verse displays the vision: of the Almighty. The verse displays the trance: the ecstasy. The verse displays the eyes: open. The vision displays the revelation given by God. The verse displays the words: heard. The verse displays the vision: seen. The verse displays the prophet: the mouth. Balaam hearing God's words and seeing the vision display the revelation from God: which heard the words of God, which saw the vision of the Almighty — the words and the vision God's own, given even to the diviner, the prophet but the mouth, the opened eyes that he might bless. The verse displays "how goodly are thy tents, O Jacob" (v.5).
5:
"How goodly are thy tents, O Jacob, and thy tabernacles, O Israel!" The witnesses note Balaam's praise of the beauty of Israel's dwellings — the crux of the goodliness of God's people. On "how goodly are thy tents, O Jacob, and thy tabernacles, O Israel," interpreters expound the blessing. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the beauty of the tents — "how goodly are thy tents, O Jacob, and thy tabernacles, O Israel": Balaam, beholding the orderly camp, broke out in praise of the beauty of Israel's dwellings; the witnesses note the significance — the goodliness of God's people in their order and blessing (the camp of Israel, set in array by their tribes around the tabernacle, a goodly and beautiful sight, the favour of God upon them visible); the lesson — the beauty of the church of God, the dwellings of his people made comely by his presence (the tents goodly not in themselves but as the dwelling of a people God had blessed; the order, the blessing, the presence of God among them their true beauty); the truth that the unwilling prophet was made to praise what he came to curse, owning the comeliness God had given; the figure — the beauty of the church, "the king's daughter is all glorious within," Psalm 45:13; the comeliness God puts upon his people, "perfect through my comeliness, which I had put upon thee," Ezekiel 16:14; the dwellings of God's people made goodly by his favour, a figure of the beauty of the church in the eyes of God, "how amiable are thy tabernacles, O LORD of hosts," Psalm 84:1; the truth that God's people are beautiful in his sight, the camp of the redeemed a goodly thing. The figure of the goodliness of the dwellings of God's blessed people.

On "how goodly are thy tents, O Jacob," the grammatical-historical and witnesses note the order. Gill and the grammatical-historical note the orderly camp, the tents in array (the beauty of the ordered camp, Psalm 84:1) — the beauty (the goodly tents).

On "and thy tabernacles, O Israel," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the dwellings. Calvin and Gill note the dwellings made comely by God's blessing and presence (the beauty of God's people, the favour upon them, Psalm 45:13; Ezekiel 16:14) — the figure (the beauty of the church, the comeliness God gives); the providence (the praise, the blessing of fruitfulness to follow, vv.6-7). The verse displays Balaam's praise of the beauty of Israel's dwellings.

The witnesses agree the substance: Balaam praised the beauty of Israel's tents and dwellings (Calvin, Gill, the grammatical-historical). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the goodliness of God's people: the beauty of the tents — the orderly camp of Israel a goodly sight, the dwellings made comely not in themselves but by the blessing and presence of God, the unwilling prophet made to praise what he came to curse, a figure of the beauty of the church made glorious by the comeliness God puts upon her (Psalm 45:13; Ezekiel 16:14; Psalm 84:1). The verse displays the goodliness crux. Balaam's praise of the beauty of Israel's dwellings displays the crux: how goodly are thy tents, O Jacob, and thy tabernacles, O Israel (Psalm 45:13; Ezekiel 16:14; Psalm 84:1) — the dwellings of God's people made goodly by his blessing and presence, the unwilling prophet praising what he came to curse, a figure of the beauty of the church in the eyes of God. The verse displays the blessing of fruitfulness and prosperity (vv.6-7).
6:
"As the valleys are they spread forth, as gardens by the river's side, as the trees of lign aloes which the LORD hath planted, and as cedar trees beside the waters." The very brief verse notes the images of Israel's fruitfulness and beauty. The grammatical voices note the figures. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "as the valleys are they spread forth, as gardens by the river's side, as the trees of lign aloes which the LORD hath planted, and as cedar trees beside the waters" — Balaam likened Israel to spreading valleys, watered gardens, fragrant aloes, and cedars by the waters; the as the valleys are they spread forth (the wide and flourishing people); the as gardens by the river's side (the watered, fruitful garden); the as the trees of lign aloes which the LORD hath planted (the fragrant trees, God's planting); the as cedar trees beside the waters (the lofty, enduring cedars); the figure (the images of fruitfulness, beauty, and permanence, Israel flourishing as God's planting; the truth that God's people are like trees planted by the waters, fruitful and enduring, "he shall be like a tree planted by the rivers of water," Psalm 1:3; the figure of the church watered by God, the trees the LORD hath planted, "that they might be called trees of righteousness, the planting of the LORD," Isaiah 61:3; the fragrance and fruitfulness of God's blessed people).

On "as the valleys are they spread forth, as gardens by the river's side," the witnesses note the fruitfulness. Gill notes Israel as spreading valleys and watered gardens (the fruitfulness, Psalm 1:3) — the fruitfulness (the flourishing people).

On "as the trees of lign aloes which the LORD hath planted, and as cedar trees beside the waters," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the planting: "the trees of lign aloes which the LORD hath planted... cedar trees beside the waters" — the fragrant aloes and lofty cedars, God's planting (the trees of righteousness, Isaiah 61:3); the figure (the church watered and planted by God); the providence (the images, the blessing of the king to follow, vv.7). The verse displays the images of Israel's fruitfulness and beauty.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the figures — "as the valleys are they spread forth, as gardens by the river's side... as cedar trees beside the waters," the figures (Keil, Gill); "as the valleys are they spread forth, as gardens by the river's side," the fruitfulness (Psalm 1:3, Gill); "as the trees of lign aloes which the LORD hath planted, and as cedar trees beside the waters," the planting (Isaiah 61:3, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the figures. The verse marks Israel: spread forth. The verse displays the gardens: watered. The verse displays the trees: God's planting. The verse displays the cedars: enduring. The figures display Israel flourishing as God's planting. The verse displays the people: fruitful. The verse displays the trees: planted. The verse displays the beauty: enduring. The images of Israel's fruitfulness and beauty display God's flourishing planting: as gardens by the river's side, as the trees... which the LORD hath planted (Psalm 1:3; Isaiah 61:3) — Israel like watered gardens and cedars by the waters, fruitful and enduring, the trees the LORD hath planted, the church watered and made fruitful by God. The verse displays the blessing of the king and the kingdom (v.7).
7:
"He shall pour the water out of his buckets, and his seed shall be in many waters, and his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted." The very brief verse notes the blessing of abundant seed and an exalted king. The grammatical voices note the kingdom. Clarke and Gill note that "he shall pour the water out of his buckets, and his seed shall be in many waters, and his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted" — Balaam foretold Israel's abundant increase and an exalted king and kingdom; the he shall pour the water out of his buckets (the abundance of water and increase); the his seed shall be in many waters (the numerous offspring); the his king shall be higher than Agag (the king above the Amalekite king); the his kingdom shall be exalted (the kingdom raised on high); the figure (the blessing of increase and dominion, the king and kingdom exalted; the foreshadowing of Israel's kingdom, and beyond it the kingdom of the Messiah, the King higher than all; the truth that God would exalt his people's king and kingdom; the figure of the kingdom of Christ exalted above all, "his kingdom shall be exalted," a kingdom that shall not be destroyed, Daniel 2:44; the abundant seed a figure of the multitude of God's people).

On "he shall pour the water out of his buckets, and his seed shall be in many waters," the witnesses note the increase. Gill notes the abundance of water and numerous seed (the increase) — the increase (the abundant seed).

On "and his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the kingdom: "his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted" — the exalted king and kingdom (the dominion, the kingdom of the Messiah, Daniel 2:44); the figure (the kingdom of Christ exalted above all); the providence (the blessing, the strength of Israel to follow, vv.8-9). The verse displays the blessing of abundant seed and an exalted king.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the kingdom — "his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted," the kingdom (Clarke, Gill); "he shall pour the water out of his buckets, and his seed shall be in many waters," the increase (Gill); "and his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted," the kingdom (Daniel 2:44, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the kingdom. The verse marks the seed: abundant. The verse displays the king: exalted. The verse displays the kingdom: raised high. The verse displays the dominion: blessed. The kingdom displays the blessing of increase and dominion. The verse displays the seed: many. The verse displays the king: higher. The verse displays the kingdom: exalted. The blessing of abundant seed and an exalted king displays the kingdom raised on high: his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted (Daniel 2:44) — Israel's abundant increase and exalted king, a foreshadowing of the kingdom of the Messiah exalted above all, the King higher than all. The verse displays the strength of Israel as a lion (vv.8-9).
8:
"God brought him forth out of Egypt; he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn: he shall eat up the nations his enemies, and shall break their bones, and pierce them through with his arrows." The very brief verse notes the strength of Israel brought out of Egypt. The grammatical voices note the strength. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "God brought him forth out of Egypt; he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn: he shall eat up the nations his enemies, and shall break their bones, and pierce them through with his arrows" — God who brought Israel out of Egypt gave them the strength of the wild ox to overcome their enemies; the God brought him forth out of Egypt (the redemption from Egypt, the ground of strength); the he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn (the strength of the wild ox); the he shall eat up the nations his enemies... break their bones... pierce them through (the victory over the foe); the figure (the strength God gave his people to overcome the nations; the truth that the redemption from Egypt was the pledge of victory, God's power their strength; the figure of the conquering people, strong in God to subdue the enemies; the strength not their own but God's, who brought them out and would give them the land, the victory of God's people over their foes by his power).

On "God brought him forth out of Egypt; he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn," the witnesses note the strength. Gill notes Israel's strength from God who redeemed them (the strength of the wild ox) — the strength (from God).

On "he shall eat up the nations his enemies, and shall break their bones, and pierce them through with his arrows," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the victory: "he shall eat up the nations his enemies... pierce them through" — the conquest of the foe (the victory by God's strength); the figure (the conquering people strong in God); the providence (the strength, the lion's rest to follow, vv.9). The verse displays the strength of Israel brought out of Egypt.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the strength — "God brought him forth out of Egypt; he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn... he shall eat up the nations his enemies," the strength (Keil, Gill); "God brought him forth out of Egypt; he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn," the strength (Gill); "he shall eat up the nations his enemies, and shall break their bones, and pierce them through with his arrows," the victory (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the strength. The verse marks Israel: brought out. The verse displays the strength: of the ox. The verse displays the nations: devoured. The verse displays the victory: God's gift. The strength displays God's power as the people's strength. The verse displays the redemption: from Egypt. The verse displays the strength: God-given. The verse displays the foe: conquered. The strength of Israel brought out of Egypt displays the victory by God's power: he shall eat up the nations his enemies, and shall break their bones — God who redeemed Israel from Egypt giving them strength to overcome the nations, the victory not their own but God's. The verse displays Israel couching as a lion (v.9).
9:
"He couched, he lay down as a lion, and as a great lion: who shall stir him up? Blessed is he that blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee." The witnesses note the lion at rest and the Abrahamic blessing reaffirmed — the crux of the inviolable blessing of God's people. On "Blessed is he that blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee," interpreters expound the blessing. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the reaffirmed promise — "Blessed is he that blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee": Balaam, describing Israel as a lion at rest none dare rouse, pronounced the very blessing God had given Abraham; the witnesses note the significance — the Abrahamic promise put in the mouth of the hireling prophet (the blessing first spoken to Abraham, "I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee," Genesis 12:3, now reaffirmed over Israel by the very man hired to curse them); the lesson — the inviolable security of God's people (); the irony — Balaam, sent to curse, compelled to bless and to pronounce a curse on all who would curse Israel, including himself and Balak; the figure — the security of the church under God, "no weapon that is formed against thee shall prosper," Isaiah 54:17; the people whom God blesses cannot be cursed, "what God hath blessed, none can reverse"; the truth that the blessing of Abraham rests on Israel and on all who are blessed in his Seed, "in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed," Genesis 12:3; Galatians 3:8-9; the figure of the inviolable favour of God upon his people, the lion at rest none can rouse. The figure of the blessing that cannot be reversed and the people that cannot be cursed.

On "he couched, he lay down as a lion, and as a great lion: who shall stir him up?" the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the lion. Keil and Ellicott note Israel as a lion at rest, whom none dare rouse (the security under God, the strength at rest) — the lion (the security of God's people).

On "Blessed is he that blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the Abrahamic blessing. Wesley and Gill note the very blessing of Abraham reaffirmed, the blessing and curse turned on those who deal with Israel (the inviolable favour, Genesis 12:3; Galatians 3:8-9) — the figure (the church that cannot be cursed, Isaiah 54:17); the providence (the blessing, Balak's anger to follow, vv.10). The verse displays the lion at rest and the Abrahamic blessing reaffirmed.

The witnesses agree the substance: Israel is a lion at rest none dare rouse, and the Abrahamic blessing is reaffirmed over them (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the inviolable blessing of God's people: the blessing reaffirmed — the very promise to Abraham put in the mouth of the hireling prophet (Genesis 12:3), the security of God's people whom none can curse, the blessing and curse turned on all who deal with them, a figure of the inviolable favour of God upon his church (Isaiah 54:17) and of the blessing of Abraham that rests on all blessed in his Seed (Galatians 3:8-9). The verse displays the inviolable-blessing crux. The lion at rest and the Abrahamic blessing reaffirmed display the crux: Blessed is he that blesseth thee, and cursed is he that curseth thee (Genesis 12:3; Galatians 3:8-9; Isaiah 54:17) — the very promise to Abraham reaffirmed by the hireling prophet, the security of God's people whom none can curse, a figure of the inviolable favour of God upon his church. The verse displays Balak's anger against Balaam (vv.10-11).
10:
"And Balak's anger was kindled against Balaam, and he smote his hands together: and Balak said unto Balaam, I called thee to curse mine enemies, and, behold, thou hast altogether blessed them these three times." The very brief verse notes Balak's anger that Balaam had blessed Israel. The grammatical voices note the wrath. Henry and Gill note that "Balak's anger was kindled against Balaam, and he smote his hands together: and Balak said... I called thee to curse mine enemies, and, behold, thou hast altogether blessed them these three times" — Balak, enraged, struck his hands together and rebuked Balaam for blessing Israel thrice; the Balak's anger was kindled... he smote his hands together (the rage); the I called thee to curse mine enemies (the purpose of the hiring); the thou hast altogether blessed them these three times (the threefold blessing); the figure (the frustrated rage of the enemy, unable to curse God's people; the truth that the purpose of the wicked against God's people is overruled and turned to blessing; the figure of the enemy's vain fury, the curse turned to blessing three times over; the powerlessness of all the malice of men against those God has blessed, the rage of Balak vain).

On "Balak's anger was kindled against Balaam, and he smote his hands together," the witnesses note the rage. Gill notes Balak's fury at the blessing (the rage) — the wrath (Balak enraged).

On "I called thee to curse mine enemies, and, behold, thou hast altogether blessed them these three times," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the threefold blessing: "thou hast altogether blessed them these three times" — the curse turned to blessing thrice (the purpose overruled); the figure (the malice of the enemy vain); the providence (the rage, Balaam's defense to follow, vv.11-13). The verse displays Balak's anger that Balaam had blessed Israel.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the wrath — "Balak's anger was kindled... thou hast altogether blessed them these three times," the wrath (Henry, Gill); "Balak's anger was kindled against Balaam, and he smote his hands together," the rage (Gill); "thou hast altogether blessed them these three times," the threefold blessing (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wrath. The verse marks Balak: enraged. The verse displays the curse: turned to blessing. The verse displays the three times: the blessing. The verse displays the enemy: frustrated. The wrath displays the vain fury of the enemy. The verse displays Balak: furious. The verse displays the blessing: threefold. The verse displays the malice: vain. Balak's anger that Balaam had blessed Israel displays the vain fury of the enemy: thou hast altogether blessed them these three times — Balak's rage at the curse turned to blessing thrice over, the malice of the enemy vain, the purpose of the wicked overruled and turned to blessing. The verse displays Balaam's defense and the warning of the fourth oracle (vv.11-14).
11:
"Therefore now flee thou to thy place: I thought to promote thee unto great honour; but, lo, the LORD hath kept thee back from honour." The very brief verse notes Balak dismissing Balaam, blaming the LORD for the lost honour. The grammatical voices note the dismissal. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "now flee thou to thy place: I thought to promote thee unto great honour; but, lo, the LORD hath kept thee back from honour" — Balak angrily dismissed Balaam, taunting that the LORD had kept him from the promised reward; the now flee thou to thy place (the dismissal); the I thought to promote thee unto great honour (the promised reward); the the LORD hath kept thee back from honour (the reward lost, blamed on God); the figure (the wages of unrighteousness withheld, the diviner's covetousness disappointed; the irony that Balak blames the LORD, who indeed kept Balaam from the reward of cursing; the truth that the covetous prophet, who loved the wages of unrighteousness (2 Peter 2:15), is denied them; the figure of the gain of wickedness frustrated, the LORD keeping his own from being cursed and the hireling from his hire).

On "now flee thou to thy place: I thought to promote thee unto great honour," the witnesses note the dismissal. Gill notes Balak dismissing Balaam, the reward withheld (the lost honour) — the dismissal (Balaam sent away).

"Nevertheless the Kenite shall be wasted, until Asshur shall carry thee away captive." The very brief verse notes the Kenite wasted and carried captive by Asshur. The grammatical voices note the captivity. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the Kenite shall be wasted, until Asshur shall carry thee away captive" — despite their stronghold, the Kenites would be wasted and carried captive by Assyria; the the Kenite shall be wasted (the wasting, the decline); the until Asshur shall carry thee away captive (the captivity by Assyria); the figure (the false security of the fastness undone, the nest in the rock no defense against God's judgment; the truth that no stronghold avails when God brings the foe; the figure of the powerlessness of earthly security against the appointed judgment; the captivity by Asshur the appointed end, the seeming strength wasted; the contrast with the true and abiding security of those who hide in God the Rock). The verse holds together the Kenite's appointed captivity.

On "nevertheless the Kenite shall be wasted," the witnesses note the wasting. Gill notes the Kenite wasted despite the stronghold (the decline) — the wasting (the Kenite undone).

On "until Asshur shall carry thee away captive," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the captivity: "until Asshur shall carry thee away captive" — the captivity by Assyria (the appointed end); the figure (the fastness no defense against judgment); the providence (the captivity, the oracle against Asshur to follow, vv.23-24). The verse displays the Kenite wasted and carried captive by Asshur.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the captivity — "the Kenite shall be wasted, until Asshur shall carry thee away captive," the captivity (Keil, Gill); "nevertheless the Kenite shall be wasted," the wasting (Gill); "until Asshur shall carry thee away captive," the captivity (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the captivity. The verse marks the Kenite: wasted. The verse displays Asshur: the captor. The verse displays the stronghold: no defense. The verse displays the judgment: appointed. The captivity displays the fastness no defense against God's judgment. The verse displays the Kenite: undone. The verse displays the captivity: appointed. The verse displays the security: false. The Kenite wasted and carried captive by Asshur displays the fastness no defense: until Asshur shall carry thee away captive — the Kenites wasted despite their stronghold, the nest in the rock no defense against God's appointed judgment, the seeming security undone. The verse displays the oracle on the ships and the fall of Asshur (vv.23-24).
23:
"And he took up his parable, and said, Alas, who shall live when God doeth this!" The very brief verse notes Balaam's lament at the judgments God would do. The grammatical voices note the lament. Clarke and Gill note that "he took up his parable, and said, Alas, who shall live when God doeth this!" — Balaam, foreseeing the great judgments and overturnings of the nations, cried out in awe, who could survive them; the he took up his parable (the last oracle); the Alas, who shall live when God doeth this! (the lament, the awe at the coming judgments); the figure (the awe before the judgments of God, the overturnings of the nations; the truth that when God arises to judge, the nations are shaken and few can stand; the figure of the terror of the day of God's judgment, "who may abide the day of his coming?" Malachi 3:2; the lament of the seer before the great acts of God in the latter days, the shaking of the nations). The verse holds together Balaam's lament.

On "he took up his parable, and said," the witnesses note the oracle. Gill notes Balaam's last oracle (the parable) — the oracle (the last parable).

On "Alas, who shall live when God doeth this!" the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the lament: "who shall live when God doeth this!" — the awe at the coming judgments (Malachi 3:2); the figure (the terror of the day of God's judgment); the providence (the lament, the fall of Asshur and Eber to follow, vv.24). The verse displays Balaam's lament at the judgments God would do.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the lament — "he took up his parable, and said, Alas, who shall live when God doeth this!" the lament (Clarke, Gill); "he took up his parable, and said," the oracle (Gill); "Alas, who shall live when God doeth this!" the lament (Malachi 3:2, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the lament. The verse marks Balaam: lamenting. The verse displays the judgments: of God. The verse displays the nations: shaken. The verse displays the awe: before God. The lament displays the awe before the judgments of God. The verse displays the seer: in awe. The verse displays the judgment: coming. The verse displays the terror: of the day. Balaam's lament at the judgments God would do displays the awe before God's judgment: who shall live when God doeth this! (Malachi 3:2) — the seer's cry of awe at the coming overturnings of the nations, the terror of the day when God arises to judge, few able to stand. The verse displays the ships from Chittim and the fall of Asshur and Eber (v.24).
24:
"And ships shall come from the coast of Chittim, and shall afflict Asshur, and shall afflict Eber, and he also shall perish for ever." The very brief verse notes the ships from Chittim afflicting Asshur and Eber. The grammatical voices note the prophecy. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "ships shall come from the coast of Chittim, and shall afflict Asshur, and shall afflict Eber, and he also shall perish for ever" — Balaam foretold ships from the western lands (Chittim) afflicting Assyria and the Hebrews, and the final perishing of the last power; the ships shall come from the coast of Chittim (the western sea power, Greece or Rome); the shall afflict Asshur, and shall afflict Eber (the affliction of Assyria and the Hebrews); the he also shall perish for ever (the final doom of the last power); the figure (the rising and falling of the empires, the western power afflicting the east, all in turn perishing; the truth that the nations rise and fall under God's hand, the last empire too perishing; the figure of the succession of the kingdoms of the world, all passing away, that the kingdom of God's King may stand; the far prophecy of the latter days, the empires shaken, the King of vv.17 alone enduring).

On "ships shall come from the coast of Chittim, and shall afflict Asshur, and shall afflict Eber," the witnesses note the affliction. Gill notes the western ships afflicting Asshur and Eber (the empires shaken) — the affliction (the western power).

On "and he also shall perish for ever," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the doom: "he also shall perish for ever" — the final perishing of the last power (the doom); the figure (all the empires passing, the King alone enduring); the providence (the prophecy, Balaam's departure to follow, vv.25). The verse displays the ships from Chittim afflicting Asshur and Eber.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the prophecy — "ships shall come from the coast of Chittim, and shall afflict Asshur... and he also shall perish for ever," the prophecy (Keil, Gill); "ships shall come from the coast of Chittim, and shall afflict Asshur, and shall afflict Eber," the affliction (Gill); "and he also shall perish for ever," the doom (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prophecy. The verse marks the ships: from Chittim. The verse displays Asshur: afflicted. The verse displays the last power: perishing. The verse displays the empires: shaken. The prophecy displays the empires rising and falling under God. The verse displays the ships: the western power. The verse displays the empires: passing. The verse displays the King: enduring. The ships from Chittim afflicting Asshur and Eber display the empires under God's hand: he also shall perish for ever — the western power afflicting the east, the empires rising and falling and all in turn perishing, that the kingdom of God's King alone may stand. The verse displays Balaam and Balak departing each to his place (v.25).
25:
"And Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his place: and Balak also went his way." The very brief verse notes Balaam and Balak parting each to his place. The grammatical voices note the departure. Ellicott and Gill note that "Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his place: and Balak also went his way" — the oracles ended, Balaam and Balak parted, each going his own way; the Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his place (Balaam's departure); the Balak also went his way (Balak's departure); the figure (the parting of the diviner and the king, their schemes against Israel defeated; the truth that all their counsel against God's people came to nothing, the blessing standing; yet Balaam, though departing, would leave the counsel of Peor behind him (Numbers 31:16), the snare that the curse could not accomplish; the figure of the enemies' designs against God's people frustrated, the blessing unbroken, though the deeper snare of seduction would follow, ch.25). The verse closes the Balaam narrative, before the sin at Peor.

On "Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his place," the witnesses note Balaam's departure. Gill notes Balaam returning to his place (the departure) — the departure (Balaam gone).

On "and Balak also went his way," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note Balak's departure: "Balak also went his way" — Balak departing (the schemes defeated, Numbers 31:16); the figure (the counsel against Israel frustrated, the snare of Peor to follow); the providence (the parting, the sin at Peor to follow, ch.25). The verse displays Balaam and Balak parting each to his place.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the departure — "Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his place: and Balak also went his way," the departure (Ellicott, Gill); "Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his place," Balaam's departure (Gill); "and Balak also went his way," Balak's departure (Numbers 31:16, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the departure. The verse marks Balaam: returning. The verse displays Balak: going his way. The verse displays the schemes: defeated. The verse displays the blessing: standing. The departure displays the counsel against Israel frustrated. The verse displays Balaam: gone. The verse displays the curse: failed. The verse displays the snare: to follow. Balaam and Balak parting each to his place displays the schemes against Israel defeated: Balaam rose up, and went and returned to his place: and Balak also went his way (Numbers 31:16) — the diviner and the king parting, their curse against God's people frustrated, the blessing unbroken, though the deeper snare of Peor would follow. The verse closes the chapter, before the sin at Baal-peor (ch.25).
